title: Thermodynamics and emergent thermomechanical response of a quantum ring with nonminimal spin--orbit coupling
authors: João A. A. S. Reis, L. Lisboa-Santos, Edilberto O. Silva
abstract: We investigate the thermodynamic and emergent thermomechanical properties of fermions confined to a one-dimensional quantum ring with effective spin--orbit interactions induced by nonminimal couplings to antisymmetric tensor fields. Using the exact spectrum obtained in the companion work, we develop canonical and grand-canonical descriptions and show that the coupling parameter~$ξ$ deforms the angular-momentum branches, reorganizing the low-energy spectrum and leaving clear signatures in the internal energy, entropy, heat capacity, and spin--orbit response functions. We also formulate an effective thermomechanical description by treating the ring circumference as a quasi-static thermodynamic variable. This leads to a pressure-like quantity and associated response coefficients, directly linked to the microscopic spectrum. In the grand-canonical ensemble, Fermi statistics strongly enhance the response, producing coupling-dependent instabilities and sign changes reminiscent of mesoscopic de~Haas--van Alphen oscillations. Finally, we introduce a phenomenological interacting extension based on an exponential resummation of the free energy, showing that collective effects can sharpen the thermomechanical response and induce anomalous thermal contraction. Our results connect spectral deformation, finite-size thermodynamics, and emergent mechanical behavior in spin--orbit-active quantum rings.
summary: This paper explores how spin-orbit coupling in a quantum ring alters its thermodynamic and mechanical properties. It shows that these interactions can induce measurable effects like mechanical instabilities and anomalous thermal contraction, providing a way to probe spin-orbit coupling through macroscopic measurements.
main_problem: Investigating how nonminimal spin-orbit coupling deforms the energy spectrum of a 1D quantum ring and how this deformation manifests in macroscopic thermodynamic and thermomechanical response functions.
main_result: The coupling parameter xi reorganizes the energy spectrum, leading to mesoscopic de Haas-van Alphen-like oscillations in the grand-canonical ensemble and even anomalous thermal contraction in an interacting model.
method: The authors use the Euler-Maclaurin summation formula to derive analytical expressions for partition functions in both canonical and grand-canonical ensembles, and introduce a phenomenological exponential resummation for interacting effects.
model_or_system: A 1D quantum ring of radius r0 containing fermions, subject to effective spin-orbit interactions induced by nonminimal couplings to antisymmetric tensor fields.
key_observables: Internal energy, entropy, heat capacity, magnetization analog, susceptibility analog, persistent spin current, effective pressure, isothermal compressibility, and thermal expansion coefficient.
important_parameters: Spin-orbit coupling strength (xi), temperature (T), chemical potential (mu), and ring circumference (L).
main_assumptions: The electrons are initially treated as non-interacting; the interacting model is phenomenological; the ring radius is treated as a quasi-static thermodynamic variable for mechanical analysis.
figures_summary: Figures demonstrate the accuracy of the Euler-Maclaurin expansion, the monotonic decrease of free energy, the sigmoid growth of entropy, the nonmonotonic behavior of magnetization and spin current, and the presence of mechanical instabilities and peaks in compressibility and expansion coefficients.
paper_structure: The paper moves from the derivation of the energy spectrum to the construction of thermodynamic partition functions, followed by the development of the thermomechanical framework and an extension into interacting systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Single-particle canonical partition function Z1 as a function of temperature. The dashed curve represents the di- rect numerical evaluation obtained by summing over a large number of energy levels, while the solid curves show succes- sive truncations of the Euler–Maclaurin expansion at zeroth, first, and second order. Already at first order, the analytic approximation is virtually indistinguishable from the exact result over the entire temperature range, confirming that the method captures both the low-temperature discreteness and the high-temperature continuum limit with remarkable preci- sion.

FIG. 3. Entropy per quantum ring as a function of tem- perature for representative values of ξ. Finite values of the effective coupling unlock the first excited states at lower tem- peratures, producing an earlier and steeper onset of entropy compared with the uncoupled case.

FIG. 2. Helmholtz free energy per quantum ring as a function of temperature for representative values of the effective cou- pling ξ. The monotonic decrease reflects the growth of ther- mally accessible microstates as temperature increases. Larger values of ξ lower the free energy throughout the plotted range, with the most pronounced separation occurring at low tem- perature, where only the first few mesoscopic branches con- tribute.

FIG. 4. Internal energy per quantum ring versus tempera- ture for several representative values of ξ. The vertical off- set between curves originates from the ξ-dependent shift of the ground-state energy, while the common positive slope at higher temperatures reflects the progressive thermal occupa- tion of excited angular-momentum states.

FIG. 6. Canonical magnetization analog M = −∂F/∂ξ as a function of temperature for representative couplings. The quantity measures the thermodynamic response of the free energy to variations of the effective Rashba parameter rather than to a physical magnetic flux.
